paper_title: BiVO4 /Bi4Ti3O12 heterojunction enabling efficient photocatalytic reduction of CO2 with H2O to CH3OH and CO

catalyst.source: BiVO4 /Bi4Ti3O12 heterojunction enabling efficient photocatalytic reduction of CO2 with H2O to CH3OH and CO
catalyst.context: BiVO4 /Bi4Ti3O12 heterojunction enabling efficient photocatalytic reduction of CO2 with H2O to CH3OH and CO

lamp.source: The UV-vis irradiation source was a 300 W Xe lamp.
lamp.context: All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation and used without further purification.Deionized water was used throughout the work. The molten salt synthesis method was used to prepare Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 , where Bi 2 O 3 and TiO 2 (anatase) were used as raw materials.Since mole ratio of Bi 2 O 3 to TiO 2 is 2:3 in Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 , Bi 2 O 3 and TiO 2 were mixed by the stoichiometric amounts.Then, NaCl, KCl and Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 were mixed at a mole ratio of 60:60:1 and continuously grinded for over 30 min with an agate mortar and pestle.The resulting mixture was then calcined at 800 C for 2 h in Muffle furnace.The mixed NaCl and KCl can be totally melted at 800 C according to the eutectic point, which is the vital steps for the generation of Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 .The resulting white product was washed with deionized water until the ion concentration gets close to zero, followed by overnight drying in an electric oven at 333 K. BiVO 4 nanomaterial was prepared by a simple in situ hydrothermal method.First, 1 mmol bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO 3 ) 3 5H 2 O) and 0.1 g sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) were separately dissolved in 6 mL and 5 mL of 2 M nitric acid solution under vigorous stirring to obtain the transparency liquid A. 1 mmol NH 4 VO 3 was dissolved in 6 mL of 2 M nitric acid to obtained the solution B. Then, the solution B was poured quickly into the solution A. A clear orange solution was formed after vigorous stirring, and then to which different molar ratios of Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 was added.In order to adjust the acid concentration to 1 M, the prepared solution was diluted by a certain amount of de-ionized water.The final resulting 35 mL solution was transferred into a 50 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 423 K for 12 h, followed by naturally cooling to room temperature.The final product was gathered by centrifugation, rinsed thoroughly with deionized water, followed by desiccation at 60 C in air overnight for further characterization.By the in situ hydrothermal method, BiVO 4 / Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 samples with different mole ratios (20 mol, 10 mol, and 5 mol ) of Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 were prepared for comparison.In addition, the nude BiVO 4 as a reference sample was prepared also by the hydrothermal method similar to the above procedure but no adding Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 .For simplicity, Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 will be abbreviated as BTO and BiVO 4 done as BVO hereinafter. Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu K1 radiation ( 1.5406A) was used to analyze the crystalline phases of the samples.Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were taken on a field emission scanning electron microscope (Nova Nano SEM 230) was used to observe the morphology.The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained via a JEOL model JEM 2010 EX instrument at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.The chemical compositions of the samples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra by a VG ESCALAB 250 XPS System with a monochromatized Al K X-ray source (15 kV, 200 W, 500 m, pass energy 20 eV).All binding energies (BEs) of the spectra were corrected to the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV of the surficial deposited carbon.UV-vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) of the samples were performed with a Cary-500 spectrophotometer, and barium sulfate was used as the reflectance standard.Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were detected at room temperature on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS 980, excitation wavelength 280 nm).In situ CO 2 -adsorbed FT-IR experiments were implemented on a Nicolet 670 FT-IR spectrometer at a resolution of 4 cm -1 and 32 scans.The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was calculated from the N 2 adsorption isothermal that was determined by a Quantachrome Autosorb IQ-C nitrogen adsorption apparatus.The photoelectrochemical measurements were carried on a conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell consisting of a working electrode (sample), counter electrode (platinum foil) and reference electrode (Ag/AgCl electrode (3.0 M KCl)).The photocurrent with light/dark short-circuit response was recorded by a BAS Epsilon workstation.Sodium sulfate electrolyte solution (0.2 M) was used as the electrolyte solution.The UV-vis irradiation source was a 300 W Xe lamp.The work functions of BiVO 4 and Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 were determined by Kelvin probe system (SKP5050, KPTechnology Ltd.) with a single-point measurement.Also, the contact potential difference (CPD) between the sample and the tip (gold, 5.1 eV) was measured in the dark.The work function of the tip was initially corrected using a gold disk.The work function of the sample was calculated by the following formula: WF of sample WF of tip CPD The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with H 2 O vapor was carried out in a 270 mL Schlenk flask reactor under atmospheric pressure at ambient temperature (298 K).The reactor was equipped with two venting valves and a sampling port sealed with a silicone rubber septum.The light source, a 300 W commercial Xe lamp with a focus intensity of 0.2 W cm -2 (300D, Perfectlight, China), was vertically placed outside the reactor.10 mg photocatalyst powder was dispersed in the bottom of reactor.Subsequently, the reactor was evacuated by a mechanical pump and filled with pure CO 2 gas at atmospheric pressure.This evacuation-filling operation was repeated several times to remove residual air. 10 L of pure water was injected into the reactor from the silicone rubber septum by a microsyringe and then the photosource was turned on.After 3 h irradiation, 0.5 mL reaction gas was sampled from the reactor and analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC-7890B, Agilent) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID), and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a chromatographic column (TDX-01).which could be seen clearly from the XRD peaks at 2 28 31(Fig.1b).It's worth noting that a new diffraction peak appears at 2 11.9withincreasing BTO content (Fig. 1b).These show that both BiVO 4 and Bi 4 Ti 3 O 12 keep their respective crystal phase in their composite, and there is interaction occurring at the interface between them.
